summary: Jeff Atwood uses the success of Desktop Tower Defense to illustrate how the internet has democratized economic opportunity for solo programmers. Paul Preece, a Visual Basic programmer with no professional game development experience, created a free Flash game that generates nearly $100,000 per year through advertising revenue alone. Atwood contrasts this with traditional publishing deals where middlemen take the lion's share—citing Roller Coaster Tycoon's creator earning $4 million while the publisher cleared $180 million. The post argues that while you might not become a millionaire, the barrier to entry for earning a solid income as an independent programmer has never been lower.
key_points: Tower Defense originated as a user-created mod for Warcraft III, demonstrating how communities can innovate beyond what game creators envision
Desktop Tower Defense generates roughly $100,000/year in ad revenue with only $130/month in hosting costs
The internet enables single programmers to bypass publishers and retain full control over their income
Traditional publishing deals are exploitative—Chris Sawyer earned $4 million from Roller Coaster Tycoon while his publisher made $180 million
Success like Paul Preece's is achievable for any motivated programmer with some luck, unlike the exceptional circumstances of traditional publishing deals
money_quotes: This is a game mode not created by Blizzard, but invented by multiplayer game modders using the tools provided with Warcraft III.
You'd be surprised how much money you can make by creating a flash game and giving it away for free on the internet.
The internet has created economic opportunity for a single programmer working alone.
No selling his soul to a publisher, no middlemen, just pure income, controlled directly by him.
Paul's success, on the other hand, is something that any programmer, with sufficient motivation and a little bit of luck, could potentially duplicate.
key_insight: The internet has lowered the barrier for solo programmers to earn substantial income independently, making modest wealth achievable without sacrificing creative control to publishers.
